subject: Physics
q:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
question:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
solution:
The RMS speed is given by 

vrms=3RTM

The average speed is given by 

vavg=8RTπM

The ratio is 

⇒vrmsvavg =3RTM8RTπM=3π8=38×227⇒vrmsvavg =3328

Comparing it with the given value 1+5x12,

vrmsvavg =1+528⇒x=28

subject: Physics
q: If the collision frequency of hydrogen molecules in a closed chamber at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{Z}$, then the collision frequency of the same system at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is :
question: If the collision frequency of hydrogen molecules in a closed chamber at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{Z}$, then the collision frequency of the same system at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is :
solution:
Assuming mean free path constant.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f} \propto \mathrm{V} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{T}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{f}_1}{\mathrm{f}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{400}} \\ & \mathrm{f}_2=\sqrt{\frac{4}{3}}=\mathrm{f}_1=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{Z}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
An ideal gas in a closed container is slowly heated. As its temperature increases, which of the following statements are true ? 

(A) the mean free path of the molecules decreases

(B) the mean collision time between the molecules decreases.

(C) the mean free path remains unchanged.

(D) the mean collision time relations unchanged.
question:
An ideal gas in a closed container is slowly heated. As its temperature increases, which of the following statements are true ? 

(A) the mean free path of the molecules decreases

(B) the mean collision time between the molecules decreases.

(C) the mean free path remains unchanged.

(D) the mean collision time relations unchanged.
solution:
As we know mean free path

λ=12NVπd2

N = no. of molecule

V = volume of container

d = diameter of molecule

Velocity constant and no. of molecules are same. So mean free path remains same. As temperature increases no. of collision increases so relaxation time decrease.

subject: Physics
q: A flask contains Hydrogen and Argon in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> by mass. The temperature of the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of the two gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>argon</mi></msub><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>hydrogen</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is: (Given : Atomic Weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>39</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn></math>)
question: A flask contains Hydrogen and Argon in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> by mass. The temperature of the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of the two gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>argon</mi></msub><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>hydrogen</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is: (Given : Atomic Weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>39</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn></math>)
solution:
The formula to calculate the average kinetic energy per molecule of a gas at any temperature T is given by

Kavg=32kBT

where, kB is Boltzmann constant.

From above equation, it can be concluded that the average kinetic energy per molecule is independent of the molecular weight of the gas.

Hence, the required ratio is 1:1.

question: A mixture of one mole of monoatomic gas and one mole of a diatomic gas (rigid) are kept at room temperature $\left(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. The ratio of specific heat of gases at constant volume respectively is:
solution: $\frac{\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}\right)_{\text {mono }}}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}\right)_{\mathrm{dia}}}=\frac{\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}}{\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{R}}=\frac{3}{5}$

subject: Physics
q:
The mean free path of a molecule of diameter $5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ at the temperature $41^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure $1.38 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$, is given as $\_\_\_\_$ m.
(Given $k_{B}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$).
question:
The mean free path of a molecule of diameter $5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ at the temperature $41^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure $1.38 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$, is given as $\_\_\_\_$ m.
(Given $k_{B}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$).
solution:
Mean free path is calculated using $\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$
Given: $d = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $T = 41°C = 314$ K, $P = 1.38 \times 10^5$ Pa, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
$\lambda = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-10})^2 \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
$= \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times 25 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
Numerator: $433.32 \times 10^{-23}$
Denominator: $1.414 \times 3.14159 \times 34.5 \times 10^{-15} = 153.18 \times 10^{-15}$
$\lambda = \frac{433.32 \times 10^{-23}}{153.18 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.83 \times 10^{-8}$ m = $2\sqrt{2} \times 10^{-8}$ m

subject: Physics
q: An ideal gas has molecules with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> degrees of freedom. The ratio of specific heats at constant pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and at constant volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></msub></mfenced></math> is:
question: An ideal gas has molecules with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> degrees of freedom. The ratio of specific heats at constant pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and at constant volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></msub></mfenced></math> is:
solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi></msub></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mi>f</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo></math><math><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math><math><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>.

subject: Physics
q: The number density of molecules of a gas depends on their distance r from the origin as, <math><mi>n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then the numer of molecules is proportional to:
question: The number density of molecules of a gas depends on their distance r from the origin as, <math><mi>n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then the numer of molecules is proportional to:
solution:
Density of molecules of gas as a of r is, n(r)=n0e-αr4
Total no. of molecules =∫0∞n(r)dv
∫0∞n0 e-αr44πr2 dr
To integrate this, substitute u=α3/4r3  this will give us dr=13α3/4r2du
Now, the integration will become, 4πn03α3/4 ∫0∞ e-u4/3du
This shows that the numer of molecules is proportional to n0α-3/4

subject: Physics
q: Consider two boxes containing ideal gases $A$ and $B$ such that their temperatures, pressures and number densities are same. The molecular size of $A$ is half of that of $B$ and mass of molecule $A$ is four times that of $B$. If the collision frequency in gas $B$ is $32 \times 10^{18} / \mathrm{s}$ then collision frequency in gas $A$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $/ \mathrm{s}$.
question: Consider two boxes containing ideal gases $A$ and $B$ such that their temperatures, pressures and number densities are same. The molecular size of $A$ is half of that of $B$ and mass of molecule $A$ is four times that of $B$. If the collision frequency in gas $B$ is $32 \times 10^{18} / \mathrm{s}$ then collision frequency in gas $A$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $/ \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
Collision frequency $(z) = \sqrt{2} \pi d^2 N \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$
Temp, N are same
$Z \propto \dfrac{d^2}{\sqrt{M}}$
$d_A = \dfrac{d_B}{2}$
$M_A = 4M_B$
$\dfrac{Z_A}{Z_B} = \dfrac{d_A^2}{d_B^2} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{M_B}}{\sqrt{M_A}} = \left(\sqrt{\dfrac{M_B}{M_A}}\right) \left(\dfrac{d_A}{d_B}\right)^2$
$= \left(\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2$
$\dfrac{Z_A}{Z_B} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{1}{8}$
$\Rightarrow Z_A = \dfrac{32 \times 10^8}{8} = 4 \times 10^8 /s$

subject: Physics
q: To raise the temperature of a certain mass of gas by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at a constant pressure,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn></math> calories of heat is required. When the same mass of gas is cooled by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at constant volume,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn></math> calories of heat is released. How many degrees of freedom does each molecule of this gas have (assume gas to be ideal)?
question: To raise the temperature of a certain mass of gas by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at a constant pressure,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn></math> calories of heat is required. When the same mass of gas is cooled by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at constant volume,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn></math> calories of heat is released. How many degrees of freedom does each molecule of this gas have (assume gas to be ideal)?
solution:
At constant pressure :

∆Q=nCP∆T ⇒   160=nCP.50  .....(1)

At constant volume

∆Q=nCv∆T ⇒  240=nCv.100......(2)

Equation (1) divided by (2)

160240=CPCV·50100

CPCV=43=1+2f

f=6

subject: Physics
q:
A 15 g mass of nitrogen gas is enclosed in a vessel at a temperature, 27oC. The amount of heat transferred to the gas, so that R.M.S. velocity of molecules is doubled, is about.
R=8.3 J K mole-1
question:
A 15 g mass of nitrogen gas is enclosed in a vessel at a temperature, 27oC. The amount of heat transferred to the gas, so that R.M.S. velocity of molecules is doubled, is about.
R=8.3 J K mole-1
solution:
The formula of root-mean-square speed, vrms=3RTM. For doubling the vrms, temperature, should be made 4-time is,  Tf=273+27×4 K=1200 K, the formula of, ∆Q=∆U+WW=0, 
⇒∆Q=∆U=nCV ∆T=1528×52R ∆T
⇒∆Q=1528×52×8.3×1200-300 J≈10 KJ.

q: A uniform heating wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The wire is then cut into half and a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied across each half separately. question: A uniform heating wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The wire is then cut into half and a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied across each half separately. The ratio of power dissipation in first case to the total power dissipation in the second case would be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
P=V2/R

P1=V2R2  ⇒  P2=V2R2    ⇒  P1+P2=4V2R

Now
P1+P2P=4V2RV2R=4

q: Two moles of a monoatomic gas is mixed with six moles of a diatomic gas. The molar specific heat of the mixture at constant volume is :
question: Two moles of a monoatomic gas is mixed with six moles of a diatomic gas. The molar specific heat of the mixture at constant volume is :
solution:
For mixture of gases, we can write

 Cv=n1Cv1+n2Cv2n1+n2

=2×32R+6×52R2+6

=94R

question: The temperature of an open room of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> increases from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> due to the sunshine. The atmospheric pressure in the room remains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub></math> are the number of molecules in the room before and after heating, then <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> will be:
solution:
Ti=273+17=290K

Tf=273+27=300K

From ideal gas equation, PV=nRT ⇒n=PVRT

nf-ni=PVR 1Tf-1Ti= -2.5×1025

subject: Physics
q: Consider a gas of triatomic molecules. The molecules  are assumed to be triangular and made of massless rigid rods whose vertices are occupied by atoms. The internal energy of a mole of the gas at temperature T is:
question: Consider a gas of triatomic molecules. The molecules  are assumed to be triangular and made of massless rigid rods whose vertices are occupied by atoms. The internal energy of a mole of the gas at temperature T is:
solution:
The formula of internal energy of a gas is given by

U=f2RT

Here R is the gas constant and T is temperature.

R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1

For the given configuration of triatomic molecules, the degree of freedom f=6

⇒ U=62RT=3RT

subject: Physics
q: During an adiabatic process, if the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature, then the ratio of $\frac{C_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}$ for the gas is :
question: During an adiabatic process, if the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature, then the ratio of $\frac{C_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}$ for the gas is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{T}^3 \\
& \mathrm{PT}^{-3}=\text { constant } \\
& \because \frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{nR}=\text { constant from ideal gas equation } \\
& (\mathrm{P})(\mathrm{PV})^{-3}=\text { constant } \\
& \mathrm{P}^{-2} \mathrm{~V}^{-3}=\text { cosntant }...(i)
\end{aligned}$
$\because$ Process equation for adiabatic process is
$\mathrm{PV}^{\mathrm{y}}=\text { constant }...(ii)$
Comparing equation (1) and (2)
$\frac{C_P}{C_V}=y=\frac{3}{2}$

subject: Physics
q:
A monoatomic gas performs a work of Q4 where Q is the heat supplied to it. The molar heat capacity of the gas will be _____ R during this transformation.

Where R is the gas constant.
question:
A monoatomic gas performs a work of Q4 where Q is the heat supplied to it. The molar heat capacity of the gas will be _____ R during this transformation.

Where R is the gas constant.
solution:
Using first law of thermodynamics,

Q=∆U+W

⇒Q=ΔU+Q4

⇒ΔU=3Q4

⇒∆U=32nRΔT=3Q4

Therefore, molar heat capacity, C=Qn∆T

⇒C=2R

If <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> for the mixture then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is,
question: Two moles of helium are mixed with n moles of hydrogen. If <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> for the mixture then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is,
solution:
γmix=1+2fmix

CpCv= fmix+2fmix=32

⇒   fmix=4

fmix= n1f1+ nf2n1+ n2

⇒4=2×3+n×52+n

⇒n=2 mole

subject: Physics
q: If the R.M.S. speed of oxygen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the R.M.S. speed of hydrogen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
question: If the R.M.S. speed of oxygen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the R.M.S. speed of hydrogen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
solution:
Recall the formula of root mean square speed, vRMS=3RTM, 

⇒vRMSO2 vRMSH2=MH2 MO2=232=14

⇒vRMSH2=4 vRMSO2=4×160=640 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: An unknown metal of mass <math><mn>192</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> heated to a temperature of <math><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> was immersed into a brass calorimeter of mass <math><mn>128</mn><mi> </mi><mi>g</mi></math> containing <math><mn>240</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> of water at a temperature of <math><msup><mrow><mn>8.4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Calculate the specific heat of the unknown metal if water temperature stabilizes at <math><msup><mrow><mn>21.5</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> <math><mo>(</mo></math> Specific heat of brass is <math><mn>394</mn><mi>J</mi><mtext> </mtext><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
question: An unknown metal of mass <math><mn>192</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> heated to a temperature of <math><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> was immersed into a brass calorimeter of mass <math><mn>128</mn><mi> </mi><mi>g</mi></math> containing <math><mn>240</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>g</mi></math> of water at a temperature of <math><msup><mrow><mn>8.4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Calculate the specific heat of the unknown metal if water temperature stabilizes at <math><msup><mrow><mn>21.5</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> <math><mo>(</mo></math> Specific heat of brass is <math><mn>394</mn><mi>J</mi><mtext> </mtext><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
Conservation of energy:

msΔT=mbsb+mwswΔTw

192×10-3s100-21.5=128×394+240×4182×10-3×21.5-8.4

s=50432+100368013.115072=916.19≃916 J kg-1 K-1

q: A <math><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> volume cylinder is filled with <math><mn>1</mn></math> mol of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> gas at room temperature <math><mo>(</mo><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> . The molecular diameter of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , and its root mean square speed, are found to be <math><mn>0.3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , respectively. What is the average collision rate (per second) for an <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> molecule?
question: A <math><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> volume cylinder is filled with <math><mn>1</mn></math> mol of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> gas at room temperature <math><mo>(</mo><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> . The molecular diameter of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , and its root mean square speed, are found to be <math><mn>0.3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , respectively. What is the average collision rate (per second) for an <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> molecule?
solution:
Average collision rate =nv- d2π
=6.023×102325×10-3×83π×200×0.3×10-92π≈4×10-12

subject: Physics
q:
The total kinetic energy of 1 mole of oxygen at 27°C is :
[Use universal gas constant (R)=8.31 J mol-1 K-1 ]
question:
The total kinetic energy of 1 mole of oxygen at 27°C is :
[Use universal gas constant (R)=8.31 J mol-1 K-1 ]
solution:
The formula to calculate the kinetic energy can be written as

K=f2nRT   ...1

Since oxygen is a diatomic gas, the number of degrees of freedom is 5.

Thus, from equation (1), it follows that

K=52×1×8.31×300 J=6232.5 J

question: At room temperature a diatomic gas is found to have an r.m.s. speed of $1930 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The gas is:

subject: Physics
q: Energy of 10 non rigid diatomic molecules at temperature $T$ is :
question: Energy of 10 non rigid diatomic molecules at temperature $T$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Degree of freedom }(f)=5+2(3 \mathrm{~N}-5) \\
& \qquad f=5+2(3 \times 2-1)=7 \\
& \text { energy of one molecule }=\frac{f}{2} K_B T
\end{aligned}$
energy of 10 molecules
$=10\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{T}\right)=10\left(\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{T}\right)=35 \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{T}$

subject: Physics
q: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
question: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
solution:
Given,

Temperature of Nitrogen gas is 

T1=300 °C=300+273=573 K

Molar mass of H2 gas is MH2=2 g

Molar mass of N2 gas is MN2=28 g

The rms speed of gas is given by

 Vrms=3RTM

Now rms speed of hydrogen gas is equal to rms speed of nitrogen gas, i.e.; VrmsH2=VrmsN2
⇒TH2TN2=MH2MN2

Substitute the given values i above equation, we get,

TH2=228×573

TH2=41 K
